dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for Breathing Problems at Apt #4 in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been getting really short of breath with wheezing. It's gotten worse all day.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your name?
David Smith.
What city are we in?
Richmond, I think.
What is your age?
Fifty-three.
Do you know why you called 911?
I can't catch my breath. It suddenly got much worse.
We'll need to do a few things to help you. Is that okay?
You're sitting up with audible wheezing. Does it feel easier this way?
Yes, only when I'm upright. If I lie down, it's worse.
Airway is patent. Any difficulty, hoarseness, or tightness in your throat?
No, not really. I can talk fine.
Partner, check for signs of respiratory distress or neck trauma.
I see wheezing and some diaphoresis. No signs of trauma here.
What do you use at home when this worsens?
Albuterol nebulizer-multiple times today. It's helping a little, not enough.
Let's start a nebulizer treatment with 5 mg Albuterol and 0.5 mg Ipratropium to help.
Baseline SpO₂ is 93% in room air. Let's patch this.
Room air SpO₂ is 93% still. Providing oxygen at 8 LPM.
Nebulizer is on for 5 mg Albuterol and 0.5 mg Ipratropium.
Keep breathing steadily. We expect the nebulizer to reduce wheezing over the next few minutes.
Is this going to fix it quickly? I've already used most of my medication.
It will help. If symptoms persist, we may also use CPAP.
Do you have any other symptoms besides wheezing and shortness of breath?
I have a dry cough that makes my chest sore.
Is the cough worse when you inhale or exhale?
It's mostly during exhalation, I think.
Respirations are 17, SpO₂ is now 97 on 8 LPM oxygen.

Mr. Smith, based on the ePCR, you initially presented with a respiratory rate of zero. This likely reflects a baseline recording while you were being assessed prior to administration of oxygen and nebulizer medications.
Mr. Smith, how would you describe the improvement in your breathing?
A little better than before, but I'm still struggling quite a bit.
Pulse is 128 right now. That's a bit high.
Breathing is at 17 per minute. No labored breathing observed.
Blood pressure is 154 over 91. Same as the past few minutes.
SpO₂ is 97 per the monitor. Improvement on 8 Lpm oxygen.
No glucose recorded at this time. We'll continue to monitor.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your medical history and about today's symptoms?
I've had COPD and asthma for years. Intubation in the past.
When did your symptoms start today?
In the morning. Started mild but has worsened all day.
The worsening-would you say it suddenly happened or as a gradual process?
Gradually. Very bad by evening.
Tell me more about your cough-do you expectorate anything?
No, it's dry. Painful, especially when exhaling.
Any known drug or food allergies?
I'm allergic to aspirin, and peanuts. Not taken aspirin recently.
Are you on any medications now?
Advair, Loratadine, Furosemide, Metformin, Neurontin.
Any other personal or family medical history we should know about?
Pneumonia as a child, current COPD, asthma, sleep apnea. Primary hypertension and sarcoidosis listed.
Mr. Smith, you're alert and oriented to person, place, time and situation. A&Ox4.
Partner, can you check for skin color, rashes, or temperature changes?
Skin is warm, no rashes or jaundice. Diaphoretic.
No signs of swelling, deformity, or trauma to the head or neck.
No visible drainage, obstruction, or foreign bodies.
Bilateral wheezing, getting slightly better with O₂ and nebulizer.
Abdomen flat and soft, no sounds or pain reported.
Both arms and legs have equal range of motion and sensation. No injuries or wounds noted.
EKG detects sinus tachycardia. Some nonspecific T wave changes noted.
We'll give you Dexamethasone IM now for airway swelling. Any concerns?
Any previous reactions or allergies?
No problems before. Proceed.
Dexamethasone 10mg administered into the upper left arm without complication.
Attaching four-lead EKG for ongoing cardiac monitoring and baseline.
Leads are in place and ready for monitoring.
The ePCR recorded successful IV start in outer left arm. But we had difficulty with poor peripheral access earlier, and needed three attempts. Let's review the 12-lead EKG now.
Completed a 12-lead EKG for full baseline information, showing sinus tachycardia.
Pulse, breathing, and other vitals now?
Pulse has dropped to 117, breathing is at 28, BP is 140 over 100, and SpO₂ of 96.
We'll transport you to VCU ED. Any objection to that?
That's the hospital I was told to go to. No issues.
Ready to move to the stair chair now?
Yes, just help steady me.
Mr. Smith, you are in the stair chair and all straps are fastened securely.
Let's confirm your breathing rate is now 33 per minute, SpO₂ is 95. We'll secure you on the stretcher once we reach the ground floor.
Vital signs are stable. Pulse is 116, breathing is 31, BP is 126 over 92, and SpO₂ is 95.
How are you feeling, Mr. Smith?
Slightly better, but still struggle when I move around.
Latest vitals-breathing at 33 per minute, other vitals unchanged.
Stretcher is now ready for transport. Patient is secured and stable.
Monitoring points again-Blood pressure is now 117 over 71.
Breathing rate is 30. Patient continues to improve slightly.
Patient presented with severe breathing difficulty and wheezing. Treated with Albuterol and Ipratropium.
GCS is 15, no trauma reported. Initial care improving, but IV placement unsuccessful due to poor peripheral access.
Diagnosing as ACOS (Asthma with COPD Overlap), and respiratory distress presenting with acute resp distress syndrome. Referred to VCU for further care.
Following the respiratory distress protocol. All interventions are in line with guidelines.
Patient is strapped and all gear is in place. Ready for transport now.
Final reading-Blood pressure at 111 over 85.
Hot transport to VCU ED. Monitoring continues to show improvement.
Patient has no pain reported throughout the duration of the call with no injury or pain to the head, neck, back, or abdomen.
You mentioned a history of diabetes non-insulin dependent. Are you managing this with lifestyle and medications?
Yes, I take Metformin and monitor my blood glucose at home.
Per the ePCR, you were diagnosed with bronchitis and viral pneumonia in the past. Has that affected your breathing today?
No, this feels like my old COPD flare-up, not like a new infection.
Thanks for the clarification. That helps our differential on the ACOS and respiratory distress.
All sets completed. Patient is stable and secured. Hot transport to VCU ED proceeding now.
